task.title: Configuring a GRETAP tunnel to transfer Ethernet frames over IPv4

li.p: Each RHEL router has a network interface that is connected to its local network, and the interface has no IP configuration assigned.
Each RHEL router has a network interface that is connected to the internet.

context.p: A Generic Routing Encapsulation Terminal Access Point (GRETAP) tunnel operates on OSI level 2 and encapsulates Ethernet traffic in IPv4 packets as described in RFC 2784.

note.p: Data sent through a GRETAP tunnel is not encrypted. For security reasons, establish the tunnel over a VPN or a different encrypted connection.

context.p: For example, you can create a GRETAP tunnel between two RHEL routers to connect two networks using a bridge as shown in the following diagram:

image.alt: Diagram of a GRETAP tunnel to connect two networks by using a bridge

step.cmd: On the RHEL router in network A:

li.p: Create a bridge interface named bridge0:
li.codeblock: # nmcli connection add type bridge con-name bridge0 ifname bridge0

li.p: Configure the IP settings of the bridge:
li.codeblock: # nmcli connection modify bridge0 ipv4.addresses '192.0.2.1/24' # nmcli connection modify bridge0 ipv4.method manual

li.p: Add a new connection profile for the interface that is connected to local network to the bridge:
li.codeblock: # nmcli connection add type ethernet port-type bridge con-name bridge0-port1 ifname enp1s0 controller bridge0

li.p: Add a new connection profile for the GRETAP tunnel interface to the bridge:
li.codeblock: # nmcli connection add type ip-tunnel ip-tunnel.mode gretap port-type bridge con-name bridge0-port2 ifname gretap1 remote 198.51.100.5 local 203.0.113.10 controller bridge0
li.p: The remote and local parameters set the public IP addresses of the remote and the local routers.

note.p: The gretap0 device name is reserved. Use gretap1 or a different name for the device.

li.p: Optional: Disable the Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) if you do not need it:
li.codeblock: # nmcli connection modify bridge0 bridge.stp no
li.p: By default, STP is enabled and causes a delay before you can use the connection.
Configure that activating the bridge0 connection automatically activates the ports of the bridge:
Active the bridge0 connection:

step.cmd: On the RHEL router in network B:

li.p: Create a bridge interface named bridge0:
li.codeblock: # nmcli connection add type bridge con-name bridge0 ifname bridge0

li.p: Configure the IP settings of the bridge:
li.codeblock: # nmcli connection modify bridge0 ipv4.addresses '192.0.2.2/24' # nmcli connection modify bridge0 ipv4.method manual

li.p: Add a new connection profile for the interface that is connected to local network to the bridge:
li.codeblock: # nmcli connection add type ethernet port-type bridge con-name bridge0-port1 ifname enp1s0 controller bridge0

li.p: Add a new connection profile for the GRETAP tunnel interface to the bridge:
li.codeblock: # nmcli connection add type ip-tunnel ip-tunnel.mode gretap port-type bridge con-name bridge0-port2 ifname gretap1 remote 203.0.113.10 local 198.51.100.5 controller bridge0
li.p: The remote and local parameters set the public IP addresses of the remote and the local routers.
Optional: Disable the Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) if you do not need it:
li.codeblock: # nmcli connection modify bridge0 bridge.stp no

li.p: Configure that activating the bridge0 connection automatically activates the ports of the bridge:
Active the bridge0 connection:
On both routers, verify that the enp1s0 and gretap1 connections are connected and that the CONNECTION column displays the connection name of the port:
li.codeblock: # nmcli device nmcli device DEVICE TYPE STATE CONNECTION ... bridge0 bridge connected bridge0 enp1s0 ethernet connected bridge0-port1 gretap1 iptunnel connected bridge0-port2

li.p: From each RHEL router, ping the IP address of the internal interface of the other router:
On Router A, ping 192.0.2.2:
On Router B, ping 192.0.2.1:
